articles.author: Lorian Belanger, Brian P. D. Hannon
articles.description: Iran launched strikes toward Israel and Gulf countries hours after an Israeli strike killed Ali Larijani, secretary of Iran’s Supreme National Security Council, and Gen. Gholam Reza Soleimani, the head of the Revolutionary Guard’s all-volunteer Basij.
articles.title: Live updates: Iran launches barrage of missiles after an Israeli strike kills its security chief

articles.author: Chan Ho-him, Sheikh Saaliq
articles.description: About 90 ships including oil tankers have crossed the Strait of Hormuz since the beginning of the war with Iran. Maritime and trade data platforms report it is still exporting millions of barrels of oil at a time when it said it closed the key waterway for vessels of the U.S. and its allies. Analysts say Iranian oil export volumes remain resilient. Many of the vessels that have passed through the Strait are so-called “dark” transits evading Western sanctions that likely have ties with Iran. More recently, vessels with ties to India and Pakistan have also successfully crossed the strait as governments stepped up negotiations.
articles.title: Dozens of ships slip through the Strait of Hormuz as Iran's oil exports get through

articles.author: Didi Tang
articles.description: Analysts say China is unlikely to help the U.S. reopen the Strait of Hormuz, and it is probably welcoming a delay in President Donald Trump’s Beijing trip. The Iran war has dragged into a third week and oil shipments through the strait are stalled. U.S. allies are also resisting deeper involvement. Experts have expressed concerns that China stands to gain if the U.S. gets bogged down in the war. Chinese diplomats are courting Middle East partners and have sent humanitarian aid to families of children and teachers killed in the bombing of an elementary school in Iran.
articles.title: China unlikely to help US reopen Hormuz as Trump’s Beijing trip slips

articles.description: An Israeli airstrike has struck an apartment building in Bachoura, central Beirut. The Israeli army had warned residents in the building to evacuate about an hour before completely flattening it as day broke.
articles.title: Israeli airstrike hits an apartment building in Bachoura, central Beirut

articles.author: Eric Tucker, David Klepper, Stephen Groves
articles.description: Top Trump national security officials will face tough questions in Congress about the war in Iran and the FBI’s ability to stop attacks at home. On Wednesday, intelligence chiefs testify before a Senate committee, and they face a House panel on Thursday. Hearings are likely to focus on the revelation that outdated intelligence likely led to the U.S. carrying out a deadly missile strike on an elementary school in Iran. The White House says it is investigating. Lawmakers also are expected to press FBI Director Kash Patel at a time when the U.S. is confronting an elevated terrorism threat.
articles.title: Top US intelligence officials will testify about Iran war

articles.author: Bill Barrow
articles.description: The Trump counterterrorism official who resigned Tuesday had been a staunch supporter of the president through his 2020 election defeat, the Jan. 6 riots and years of conservative media advocacy and failed congressional bids. But the president's war in Iran and his alliance with Israel against the Islamic clerics who led the Tehran government were too much for Joe Kent. Kent's resignation deprives Trump of a once-staunch ally and also highlights the former Army Green Beret's previous ties to antisemitism and right-wing extremism. Kent had 11 combat tours, mostly in Iraq, but became an opponent of U.S. interventionism after his wife was killed in Syria in 2019.
articles.title: What to know about Joe Kent, Trump's former counterterrorism chief

articles.author: Christopher Rugaber
articles.description: Fed Chair Jerome Powell is almost certain to announce Wednesday that the central bank has kept its key rate unchanged for the second straight meeting at about 3.6%. But the Fed will also release a set of quarterly projections, and they could alter their forecast of one rate cut this year to zero. While such a change might seem minor, it would be a major course correction after 18 months of on-again, off-again rate cuts.
articles.title: Federal Reserve could signal no interest rate cuts this year in wake of Iran war

articles.author: Sophia Tareen
articles.description: Illinois voters are deciding primaries for six open U.S. House and Senate seats that will spur a new generation of leadership in the state’s heavily Democratic congressional delegation. The retirement of Democratic Sen. Dick Durbin has triggered a competitive campaign to find a replacement. Among the candidates in Tuesday's Democratic primary are two sitting House members and the lieutenant governor. The race is also a test of the influence of Democratic Gov. JB Pritzker. House retirements created open seats across the Chicago area. The American Israel Public Affairs Committee and PACs supporting the cryptocurrency and AI industries also have spent big on several of the contests. The stakes are high, with most primary winners in the Democratic stronghold expected to win in November.
articles.title: Illinois voters pick new slate of Democratic leaders in Tuesday primary

articles.author: Thomas Beaumont, Jesse Bedayn
articles.description: President Donald Trump says he plans to endorse a candidate in the Texas Republican Senate runoff to end the painful primary. But the Tuesday deadline passed for the candidates to withdraw from the ballot. Neither Sen. John Cornyn nor state Attorney General Ken Paxton bowed out of the runoff, after both failed to get a majority of votes in the March 3 primary. Instead, both candidates’ campaigns and the super PACs supporting them launched new attack advertisements. It all means two more months of a bitter and costly campaign for Republicans. The winner will face Democratic nominee state Rep. James Talarico.
articles.title: Cornyn and Paxton both remain in Republican Senate runoff in Texas

articles.author: Patrick Whittle
articles.description: Democratic U.S. Senate hopefuls Gov. Janet Mills and Graham Platner are sparring over an advertisement released by the Mills campaign criticizing Platner’s past online comments. Mills and Platner are competing for the Democratic nomination to take on Republican Sen. Susan Collins, whose seat is key for the Democrats’ hopes to take back the Senate. The Mills campaign released an ad on Tuesday in which women read some of Platner’s old social media posts that were dismissive of sexual assault. Platner's campaign dismissed the ad as a desperate attack from Mills.
articles.title: Democratic US Senate race heats up as Mills targets Platner's online comments
